title: A Loving Appeal on This Day!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Today, 10 August 2014.
How many memories arise at the sight of this date!
On this very day, seventy-two years ago, on 10 August 1942, *Murasoli*, praised by everyone as my first child, was born. That was the day the journal began. What a history *Murasoli* has!

The newspaper now eagerly supported by Udanpirappus across Tamil Nadu first appeared in Tiruvarur as a handbill. At the beginning, thousands of copies were printed each month and sent to branches of the Dravidian movement in every town. Donations collected by those branches enabled the handbill to appear. *Murasoli* helped the movement grow, and the movement helped *Murasoli* grow. It also helped develop my craft as a writer. Today *Murasoli* has many writers, but I am its permanent writer for life. "Cheran" was my pen name. Its first secretary, Thennan, and treasurer, T. S. Gopal, are no longer with us.
For the memory of today's young people, I reproduce here a *Murasoli* handbill I issued on 5 October 1944:

> **Birth of Murasoli: E. V. R. Year 67** > Drum: 2. Sound: 7. Purattasi 20, 1944. > Writer: Cheran. Secretary: Thennan. > **Varna, or Honour?** > > The Dravidian comrade, once intoxicated, has awakened today. He saw the chapter in which valour was sold and sprang up in shame. Its echo is the Self-Respect Movement! The Tamil who has seen a new era! The Tamil who has seen Periyar's era! He has begun to ask: "Will a net be cast once again over the life of such a Tamil?" > > So long as certain so-called progressives regard attaching tails such as Pillai, Mudaliar, Reddiar and Desikar to their names as the summit of elegant living, even our tongues hesitate a little to condemn the fourfold varna order. We have no business with barbarians who say, "Being called a fool suits me; that itself is my title of Sir." Yet does compassion not arise naturally? > > Having discovered that only by creating gullible people can they live in pleasure, they call the long-dead corpse of varnashrama merely unconscious, sprinkle water upon it and open its eyes. That spectacle is to be staged at Chidambaram on 8, 9 and 10 October. At Thillai a grave will be dug for this attempt to dress a corpse as a bridegroom and extract money from the people. Assemble and come to cleanse this filth! > > There is no time now merely to sing a refrain blaming the enemy. Hurry to Thillai with the resolve for liberation! In the ancient Tamil land that lived with dignity, singing many *paranis*, though a few shameless, selfish brothers gather and perform contortions in a futile attempt to establish varna: > > At the very summit of death, to guard honour, > Warrior Tamil, fight! Varnashrama must fall!
The *Murasoli* that began in this way is today one of the weapons carried by the soldiers of liberation.

*Murasoli*'s golden-jubilee celebration was held on 10 August 1992 at Kalaignar Marriage Hall, Anna Arivalayam, Chennai, under the chairmanship of the General Secretary, the Professor. Brother Murasoli Maran delivered the welcome address, and I released the golden-jubilee volume and spoke. With my own hands I honoured with shawls my friend Tiruvarur Thennan, who stood beside me when I began *Murasoli*; Karunai M. Jamal, owner of Karunanidhi Press in Tiruvarur, who first printed it; its early administrator C. T. Moorthy, known as CTM; Murasoli Sornam; and manager Periyannan.

Speaking at the celebration, the General Secretary announced the decision taken that morning by district secretaries to establish a trust to keep *Murasoli* running. V. P. Singh sent a handwritten message of greeting in English, signing his name in Tamil.
In the poem I wrote for the golden-jubilee volume, I said:

> Drinking the milk of life from Mother Anjugam, > Crawling in joy towards Ayya's principles, > Growing under Anna's boundless affection, > Raising, with youthful vigour, an army against Hindi, > I came to *Murasoli* with the drive of an ideal. > > O Tamil, who gave us the threefold wisdom > Along the path of the ancient Valluvar, > Never leaving me even for an instant, > Holding and protecting me, on your behalf > I dedicate this golden-jubilee volume of the battle drum > To every Udanpirappu who rises with a roar; > To Mother, Father Periyar and Anna, forever flowering in my heart; > And to the elders and learned people who offer their blessings.
In his jubilee greeting, the General Secretary wrote:

> *Murasoli*, the shield of the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam, is also the plough in the field that cultivates Tamil. Bearing countless governmental obstructions, court cases and unforeseen losses, *Murasoli* marches proudly like a battle-scarred elephant. On its golden jubilee I wish its journey many, many more years. May you live to see the day when the honour lost by our people and language is recovered and Tamils find the way to live with heads held high. I turn the pages after offering my greeting and see in it the very likeness of Kalaignar M. K.'s face. I greet you again: may you live forever.
The *Murasoli* that grew in all these ways turns seventy-two today. I have completed ninety years and entered my ninety-first. Writing on 11 March 1984, I said:

> It staggers, takes faltering steps, treads upon fire, falls and rises. Yet in no condition has it made its adopted principles kneel; never has it thought of drawing back the foot placed forward. *Murasoli* is my first child. Yes, like the Pandyan who won the battle of Thalaiyalanganam, that beloved first child, anklets ringing on its feet, was sent to face many enemies in the field with the words: "Go forth, my son, towards the battlefront!"
To honour *Murasoli*'s birthday today, brother Kavichudar Kavithaipithan has written:

> *Murasoli* is a great feast for our feelings! > *Murasoli* is Tamil medicine for our intellect! > *Murasoli* is our weapon, the address > And identity of the Tamil people, our growth and vigour! > *Murasoli* is the artery that moves our body; > *Murasoli* is the linchpin of the movement's chariot! > I call *Murasoli* our life, without hesitation; > Without *Murasoli*, there is no movement for us!

Across these seventy-two years of growth, there have been moments of weariness. But its head has never hung. Today is the seventy-second birthday of *Murasoli*, which has been the great support behind my call to stand upright and beat the drum. This growth was made possible by the Udanpirappus dearer to me than life, who carry it as both sword and shield while conducting the movement. I thank them all at this moment.

In these seventy-two years, *Murasoli* has known countless political, family and movement events; countless political turns; periods of tenderness and periods of bitterness. It knows every change of climate. It has travelled over stones and thorns, stood forever in the field, crossed wild rivers and overcome packs of fierce beasts. Let me recall one event.

During the 1991 general election, young Indian leader Rajiv Gandhi contested on the side opposed to the DMK. After his death, AIADMK members and antisocial elements incited grieving Congress workers and exploited that moment of crisis to invade and create havoc in DMK homes and offices. Large parts of the *Murasoli* office were set on fire. Old files preserved for many years were reduced to ash, and many parked vehicles were burned.

Brother Kalanithi Maran, who was then looking after the office, heard the news, took a scooter parked outside my house and went to see the burning building. He could not even enter, and the scooter was burned before his eyes. The printing machines were destroyed so thoroughly that the paper could not continue to be printed there.

Our opponents thought, "The story is over." Yet that very *Murasoli* continues its pure service today as an unending story. My loving appeal on this day is that the movement's Udanpirappus continue, as always, to pour their support upon my first child, *Murasoli*, as it enters its seventy-second year.
With affection,
M. K.
11 August 2014
